coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots reinforced polypyrrole nanowire via electrostatic self-assembly strategy for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Jian, Xuan
Li, Jia-gang
Yang, Hui-min
Cao, Le-le
Zhang, Er-hui
Liang, Zhen-hai
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) reinforced polypyrrole nanowire (PPy-NW) was constructed via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. The as-made CQDs/PPy-NW composite demonstrated superior electrochemical properties benefited from a dotted line structure with large specific area, more active sites and outstanding electronic conductivity. As the results, the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode displayed a specific capacitance of 306 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and retained 66.8% capacitance when the current density increased by 80 times (from 0.5 to 40 A g−1). In addition, the as-fabricated symmetric supercapacitor was able to offer a large areal capacitance (248.5 mF cm−2 at 0.2 mA cm−2) and good rate capability, the supercapacitor still retained good cycling stability even at a high current density of 5 mA cm−2 (85.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles). In conclusion, the good electrochemical properties suggest a great potential of the CQDs/PPy-NW in the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs and CQDs/PPy-NW composite
The surface morphology and inner structures of CQDs/PPy-NW composite
Electrochemical characterization of CQDs/PPy-NW symmetric supercapacitor
Ragone plot of CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of electrostatic self-assembly synthesis of CQDs/PPy nanocomposite for symmetric electrochemical supercapacitor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM image of CQDs and inset HRTEM (high-resolution TEM) of individual an CQDs; (B) PL study of CQDs with different excitation wavelengths; (C) The corresponding size distribution of CQDs; (D) AFM image of CQDs with the height profile along a black line show in. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the filtrates obtained after every adsorption cycle, inset is the digital image of the filtrates showing the color changed after every filtration period. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of pure PPy-NW (A) and (B). Different magnification TEM images of CQDs/PPy-NW (C–D) and insets of (C) show an enlarged zone of the CQDs/PPy-NW. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FTIR spectra of CQDs, pure PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW hybrid; (B) Raman profiles and (C) XRD spectra of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite; (D) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite and the inset is pore-size distribution curves of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs, PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite; C1s spectra of CQDs (B), PPy-NW (C), CQDs/PPy-NW (D); N1s spectra of PPy-NW (E), CQDs/PPy-NW (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cyclic voltammogram curves of PPy-NW (A) and CQDs/PPy-NW composite (B) electrodes with potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) from −0.4–0.6 V; Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of PPy-NW (C) and CQDs/PPy-NW composite (D) at different current densities; (E) Specific capacitance of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes at different current densities; (F) Nyquist plots for PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode.(A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram demonstrating the capacitive properties for CQDs/PPy-NW composite. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of CQDs/PPy-NW symmetric supercapacitor. (A) Cyclic voltammogram curves at different scan rates; (B) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves from 0.4 to 4 mA cm−2; (C) Areal capacitance of the supercapacitor at various current densities; (D) Nyquist plots; (E) Plots of the Cre and Cim of the specific capacitance vs. frequency; (F) Cycle stability and inset is 1st-3rd and 4998th–5000th cycles of charge-discharge curves. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Ragone plots of CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor device: (A) On the basis of the total mass of the active material; (b) On the basis of the geometric area of the supercapacitor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots reinforced polypyrrole nanowire via electrostatic self-assembly strategy for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) reinforced polypyrrole nanowire (PPy-NW) was constructed via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. The as-made CQDs/PPy-NW composite demonstrated superior electrochemical properties benefited from a dotted line structure with large specific area, more active sites and outstanding electronic conductivity. As the results, the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode displayed a specific capacitance of 306 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and retained 66.8% capacitance when the current density increased by 80 times (from 0.5 to 40 A g−1). In addition, the as-fabricated symmetric supercapacitor was able to offer a large areal capacitance (248.5 mF cm−2 at 0.2 mA cm−2) and good rate capability, the supercapacitor still retained good cycling stability even at a high current density of 5 mA cm−2 (85.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles). In conclusion, the good electrochemical properties suggest a great potential of the CQDs/PPy-NW in the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have been focusing on supercapacitors as novel energy storage devices because of the following reasons: portable electronic device, fast charging/discharging process, high energy density and long lifetime [1,2]. Based on the energy storage mechanism and electrode materials used, the electrochemical capacitor can be classified into two types, namely, electric double layer capacitor (EDLC) and pseudo-capacitor. In general, carbon materials with large surface area as well as excellent electrical conductivity, such as graphene [3], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [4] and carbonfibers [5], are commonly used for EDLC. The pseudo-capacitors are based on the redox active electrode materials, involving fast and reversible Faradaic reactions of electroactive species, such as conducting polymers and various transition metal oxides [6–9].

Among these electrode materials, conducting polymers, such as polypyrrole (PPy) [10], polythiophene [11] and polyaniline (PANI) [12], have been intensively studied due to their excellent electrochemical performance, large storage capacity and reversibility. In particular, as an important and popular conducting polymer, PPy is widely applied for its predominances such as easy synthesis, environment stability and low cost. Moreover, it is noteworthy that recent researches have been devoted to preparing different nanostructures of PPy (such as nanowire [13], nanotube [14] and nanofiber [15]) in an energy storage system to reinforce capacitance. However, the poor cycle-life is a difficult problem, which restricts the practical application of PPy. Therefore, carbon materials-doped PPy composite are being studied to overcome this disadvantage. Zhou et al. fabricated graphene-doped PPy as efficient electrode material for supercapacitor [16]. Tsai's group demonstrated a novel method for the incorporation of graphene oxide into PPy by direct electropolymerization which is capable of supercapacitor property [17]. Fu et al. reported that CNT-embedded PPy nanowires can be used as high power density supercapacitor electrode material [18].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as novel zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials have been attracting attentions for their potential as a promising material for energy storage because of their high surface area, good electrochemical conductivity and well dispersed in various solvents [19–23]. The study by Zhu [24] showed that CQDs/RuO2 network is an excellent electrode material for a supercapacitor. Yan's group successfully fabricated all solid-state GQDs (graphene quantum dots)//PANI asymmetric microsupercapacitor by direct electrochemical method, which exhibited desirable electrochemical capacitance performance [25]. Qu et al. reported that GQDs electrodeposited 3D graphene composite as electrode material showed an excellent capacitance of 268 F g−1 compared with pure 3D graphene (136 F g−1) [26].

On the basis of these studies, we attempt to use CQDs reinforced PPy nanowire network (CQDs/PPy-NW) via electrostatic self-assembly strategy as high-performance energy storage electrode material. Fig. 1 presents the schematic diagram of preparation of CQDs/PPy-NW composite. As shown in the scheme we can forecast that the good conductivity of CQDs would reduce the charge transfer resistance and increase the rate of electrochemical reaction in the PPy-NW [27,28]. As the result, thanks to the dotted line structure of the composite, the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode demonstrates a high performance capacitance, and the as-fabricated symmetric supercapacitor with two CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes offers high areal capacitance (248.5 mF cm−2) and good cycling capability (85.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and CQDs/PPy-NW composite
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Atomic force microscope (AFM) images of CQDs were taken with a Tecnai G220 (FEI, USA) operating at 200 kV and a multimode nanoscope IIIa controller (Veeco, USA), respectively. The surface scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of CQDs/PPy composite morphology were investigated with a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan). Specific surface area was calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen adsorption/disorption measurement (Norcross ASAP 2020, USA). The CQDs/PPy composite structural information was determined from a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR-Prestige-21, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using a standard KBr pellet method. The composite crystalline phases was examined by an X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument analysis (Rigaku, D/max-2500) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15,406 nm) within the 2θ range from 10°–90°. Raman spectrum was taken with laser confocal micro-Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR800, France). Elemental compositions of the samples were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA).

All electrochemical experiments in this work were performed using a V3 electrochemical workstation (Princeton, USA). A three-electrode system, including an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE), a Pt wire was applied as the counter electrode (CE) and CQDs/PPy-NW modified glassy carbon electrodes (GCE), were used as the working electrode (WE) in an electrolyte of 1.0 M KCl. In the two-electrode system, the capacitor cell (Fig. S1) consisting of CQDs/PPy-NW dropped onto ITO conducting glasses as the cathode and anode, respectively. Briefly, as-prepared CQDs/PPy-NW was dispersed in dimethylformamide (DMF) and ultrasonicated to form a uniform suspension (5 mg mL−1). Then a total of 100 μL of well dispersed suspension was applied onto the ITO-coated glass (1 cm × 1 cm conductive areas) and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. At last, the two CQDs/PPy-NW electrodes were separated by a piece of filter paper soaked with 1.0 M KCl electrolyte. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurement were performed at the various scan rates in the potential range of 0–0.8 V. The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) was conducted at different current densities. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in the frequency range of 0.01–100 000 Hz with an AC amplitude of 5 mV.

Pyrrole (Py, A.R.), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, A.R.), Ammonium persulphate (APS, A.R.) and Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (CTAB, A.R.) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled ultrapure water (DI water) with an electric resistance >18.3 MΩ was used for all solution preparation.

CQDs and PPy-NW were obtained using methods from previous literatures [18,29]. The as-produced watersoluble CQDs were synthesized through direct electrochemical approach. Briefly, two graphitic rods were set as anode and cathode at 30 V potential with continuous electrolysis for 120 h. Then the dark yellow solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitate.

To prepare the PPy-NW, 200 mg CTAB as soft template was dispersed in 1.0 mol L−1 HCl solution, and 200 mg pyrrole monomer was added slowly with constant stirring half an hour at room temperature. Then, 1 mol L−1 HCl containing 680 mg APS was added to the above-mentioned solution as an oxidant. The mixture was stirred in an ice bath for 12 h. The obtained precipitate was washed with DI water and ethanol for several times.

The dotted line structure of CQDs/PPy-NW was synthesized via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. Firstly, the different amounts PPy-NW (12.5, 25, 50 and 100 mg) were added to 100 mL CQDs solution (1 mg mL−1) under ultrasonication for 2 h. Then, the dispersion was filtrated through a membrane (0.22 μm), thereby resulting in a black precipitate. To adsorb all the CQDs, filtration was repeated until no UV–Vis absorbance signal can be detected from the filtrate. According to the different mass ratio of added CQDs and PPy-NW, the finally products were named as 8-CQDs/PPy-NW, 4-CQDs/PPy-NW, 2-CQDs/PPy-NW and 1-CQDs/PPy-NW, respectively.

Fig. 2 demonstrates typical characterization results of as-prepared CQDs. The TEM images in Fig. 2(A) and (C) show that the CQDs are well-dispersed with a relatively narrow lateral size in the range from 3 to 8 nm. The representative high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the individual CQDs in the inset of Fig. 2(A) indicates that resultant CQDs are highly ordered with an interlayer spacing of 0.32 nm [29,30]. The formation of as-prepared CQDs is further confirmed by photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 2(B), the maximum fluorescence intensity of the CQDs is observed at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm Red-shift occurred with increasing the excitation wavelength, which is likely due to the effect of size and surface functional groups [24]. Furthermore, AFM image of Fig. 2(D) was used to confirm the CQDs with a narrow height distribution ranging from 0.34 to 2.3 nm, implying that most of CQDs consist of ca. 1 to 4 atomic layers [29,30].

Herein, we proposed a simple approach for preparing a dotted line structure of CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. Fig. 1 clearly displays the preparation process of the CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite. The basic technical principle is as follows. Firstly, the PPy-NW can easily disperse in water because of their positive charge [27], the zeta potential of +51.7 mV (Fig. S2(A)). However, CQDs possess negative polarity (the zeta potential of - 45.6 mV, Fig. S2(B)) because of abundant of oxygen groups such as hydroxyl (OH) and carbonyl (CO) on the CQDs edge [31]. Thus, theoretically, CQDs can easily be adsorbed on the surface of PPy-NW via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. To fully ensure that all the CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of PPy-NW through this approach, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the filtrates were monitored after every filtration period. As expected, in Fig. 3 , there is no UV–Vis absorbance signal detected from the filtrates after six times of the adsorption process, thereby indicating that all the CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of PPy-NW via electrostatic self-assembly strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface morphology and inner structures of CQDs/PPy-NW composite
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of pure PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composites was further characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The images in Fig. 4 (A) and (B) show a thin layer network of pristine PPy-NW and several micrometers in length, in good accordance with the previous report [18,27,32]. As shown in Fig. 4(C) and (D), a few CQDs were absorbed on the surface of PPy-NW through electrostatic self-assembly strategy. In contrast, the TEM images of Fig. S3 display a lot of CQDs were evenly attached to the surface of PPy-NW and the PPy-NW surface became rough. Besides, the CQDs/PPy-NW shows a thin film-like structure on the surface of PPy-NW in the images of Fig. S3(B). This can be regarded as direct evidence for the adsorption of CQDs on the PPy-NW through electrostatic self-assembly strategy. Although it is not accurate to determine the pore size by the TEM images, the interlayer spacing were approximately 0.342 nm could be seen in the inset, corresponding well to the (002) interlayer spacing of graphitic carbon [24,26,28,33].

Fig. 5 (A) demonstrates the FTIR spectra of CQDs, pure PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite. In the CQDs spectrum, the strong bands at 3450 and 1724 cm−1, which are corresponding to hydroxyl (OH) and carbonyl (CO), respectively. The bands at 1140 and 1031 cm−1 are assigned to the C-O groups. The abundance of oxygen groups on the edge of CQDs was in agreement with the results of previous studies [29]. In the pure PPy-NW spectrum, the characteristic peaks are observed at 1548 and 1462 cm−1 with regards to the pyrrole ring. The bands at 1305 and 1038 cm−1 are assigned to C-N stretching vibrations and C-H deformation vibrations, respectively. The bonds observed at 1174 and 901 cm−1 indicate the doping state of PPy-NW. In addition, the peak at 780 cm−1 verifies the presence of polymerized pyrrole [18,32]. In contrast, the spectrum of CQDs/PPy-NW exhibits a weak peak at 1724 cm−1 corresponding to carbonyl group (CO) stretching vibration, which is in line with the CQDs curve, indicating that CQDs was successfully adsorbed on the surface of PPy-NW via electrostatic self-assembly.

As shown in Fig. 5(B) and (C), Raman and XRD spectroscopy were further used to characterize the crystal structure of pure PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite, respectively. As compared in Fig. 5(B), Raman spectra of both the pure PPy-NW and the CQDs/PPy-NW generally present two prominent Raman-active peaks of the “disorder” D-band (∼1344 cm−1) and the “crystalline” G-band (∼1575 cm−1) [33]. Additionally, the I D/I G values of 0.93 and 0.85 for PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW, respectively, suggesting that the defects of the pure PPy-NW were removed during the electrostatic adsorption process of CQDs. The XRD patterns as shown in Fig. 5(C), a broad peak at 2θ = 25.9 °can be observed in the curves of both the PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW, implying that the amorphous state of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW in nature [34].

BET specific surface area measurements were carried out to determine the surface area and pore-size distribution of the pure PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite. As presented in Fig. 5(D), the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms demonstrate obvious hysteresis loops implying the isotherm curves of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite can be classified as type Ⅳ under the IUPAC classification, and the inset of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution which might also be ascribed to the presence of mesopores between nanostructures [27]. Particularly, in the inset of CQDs/PPy-NW composite pore-size distribution, the presence of mesopores with diameter from 0 to 50 nm is increased when the CQDs were uniformly adsorbed on PPy-NW surface, presumably because of the formation the interstices between adsorbed CQDs and PPy-NW [34]. As the results, the BET surface of area of CQDs/PPy-NW is calculated to be 318.5 m2 g−1, which is larger than that of PPy-NW (62.5 m2 g−1), implying that a much larger electrode/electrolyte interface that would contribute to the larger capacitance performance [26]. All the above-mentioned results demonstrate that the CQDs/PPy-NW have larger surface area, a further reminder of the TEM results.

XPS measurements were carried out to characterize the chemical environment of CQDs, PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite. The XPS survey spectrum of CQDs, PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW are displayed three major peaks in Fig. 6 (A), revealing that the main elements on the as-prepared electrode material are C, N and O. The detailed chemical state of CQDs was characterized by deconvolution of C1s spectra. As seen in Fig. 6(B)–(D), the high-resolution shows the deconvoluted C1s spectrum of CQDs, PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite, respectively. The presence of CC, CO, COH and COOH bonds in Fig. 6(B), indicating the as-made CQDs were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups [35]. For the C1s XPS spectrum of the PPy-NW, three component peaks centered at 284.8, 286 and 287.5 eV, which originates from sp2 carbon, CN and CO, respectively. In Fig. 6(D) obviously revealed that the C1s core level XPS spectrum of CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite combines the characteristics of both CQDs and pure PPy-NW, and could be fitted into four component peaks located at 284.8, 285.8, 286.8 and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon, CN, CO and COOH, respectively [16,36].

Moreover, a deeper insight into the XPS spectrum in the region of 396–410 eV, N1s of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW is displayed in Fig. 6(E) and (F). For the N1s XPS spectrum of PPy-NW, the major peak component at a binding energy (BE) of 400.8 eV is assigned to NH, the high BE tail which originates from the positively charged nitrogen (-N+) and the low BE component at 398.8 eV which can be attributed to N [16,36,37]. Similarly, N1s XPS spectrum of CQDs/PPy-NW composite also showed three components peaks located at 398.4, 400.7 and 402.2 eV, which are assigned to N, NH and N+, respectively. According to N1s XPS spectrum of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite, we can observe that the higher ratio of N+/N from CQDs/PPy composite (0.545) than pure PPy-NW (0.412). Such difference might be due to the negatively charged CQDs, which acted as the counter anion and was incorporated into PPy-NW to balance the positive charges [27].

The electrochemical capacitance of the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes is evaluated in a three-electrode cell in 1.0 M KCl electrolyte with a Pt counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Fig. 7 (A) and (B) show the CV curves of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes at different scan rates. In comparison with pure PPy-NW, the CV curves of CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode have larger area, mirror-image symmetry and a quasi-rectangular shape, revealing that the adsorption of CQDs well improves the capacitance of the PPy-NW. Besides, Fig. 7(B) demonstrates that the CV curves area of CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode increased gradually with increasing scan rate, and even at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, it still maintained the rectangular shape, indicating that the CQDs/PPy-NW composite has fast charge-discharge property. Its high capacitance is contributed by the dotted line structure of CQDs/PPy-NW facilitates the transport of the electrolyte ions and the pseudocapacitance of the PPy-NW. In addition, we have further studied the effect of CQDs content on the capacitance of CQDs/PPy-NW. Fig. S4 shows that the CV curves of 1-CQDs/PPy-NW, 2-CQDs/PPy-NW, 4-CQDs/PPy-NW and 8-CQDs/PPy-NW have quasi-rectangular shape at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 compared with pure PPy-NW. Fig. S5 summarizes the specific capacitances of CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes with different CQDs weight ratio, displaying that the 4-CQDs/PPy-NW had the highest specific capacitance of 248 F g−1 (obtained from GCD curves at 10 A g−1). The reason is the adsorption of CQDs on the surface of PPy-NW accelerates the charge transfer rate, but too much amount CQDs were coated would degrade the electrical conductivity [37], thereby leading to the decrease of the capacitance of the CQDs/PPy-NW composite.

As shown in Fig. 7(C) and (D), the PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes are investigated by GCD test at different current densities. It is worth noticing that the specific capacitances value decreases upon increasing the current density, this observation can be ascribed to the presence of inner active sites that cannot be fully complete the redox transitions at higher current density. The specific capacitances for PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW are summarized in Fig. 7(E). The CQDs/PPy-NW retained 66.8% capacitance, which is larger than that retained by pure PPy-NW (6.9%) when the current density increased by 80 times (from 0.5 to 40 A g−1). Furthermore, Fig. S6 displays that the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode has lower iR drop compared with the PPy-NW at a current density of 10 A g−1, thereby further confirming that an appropriate amount CQDs adsorbed on the surface of PPy-NW would reduce the internal resistance to less energy waste.

Electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) is a powerful technique that can be used to further investigate the electrochemical behavior between the electrode and the electrolyte. Fig. 7(F) present the Nyquist plots for PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode. As expected, the PPy-NW electrode has a larger semicircle, indicating that PPy-NW has larger charge-transfer resistance (R ct). On the contrary, because of the dotted line structure, the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode has small R ct, indicating rapid charge/ion transfer for the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode. Additionally, Fig. 8 illustrates the schematic of charge transport in PPy-NW and fast electron transfer on the CQDs. The charging-discharging mechanism for as-made CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode can be written as following:
(1)
PPy +
nCl
−
↔
PPy
n +
(
Cl
−
)
n
+
ne
−
( p − doping )
Which suggesting that the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode can be classified into a capacitor configuration (TypeⅠ), using the same p-dopable polymer for both electrodes [38,39].

In conclusion, the excellent performance of CQDs/PPy-NW could be attributed to the follow reasons: (1) As revealed in the surface of the CQDs/PPy-NW composite (Fig. 4(C)) and the BET results (Fig. 5(D)), using of the PPy-NW network as scaffolding can provide large specific active area to improve the charge transport; (2) The dotted line structure of CQDs/PPy-NW can further reduce the charge transfer resistance, which can ensure rapid charge/ion transfer and increase the rate of electrochemical reaction in the pure PPy-NW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of CQDs/PPy-NW symmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above studies of PPy-NW and CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes, the CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode demonstrates the best overall performance. Thus, the symmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated with two CQDs/PPy-NW electrodes by using a piece of filter paper soaked with 1.0 M KCl electrolyte as separator. Fig. 9 (A) shows the CV curves of the supercapacitor at various scan rates of 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200 mV s−1. The nearly rectangular shape of CVs reveals that the CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor has ideal capacitive behavior. Additionally, even at high scan rate, the CV curves still maintained the quasi-rectangular shape, implying the fast charge-discharge property of the CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor. The GCD curves of the CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor at different current densities of 0.4, 0.8, 1, 2 and 4 mA cm−2 are shown in Fig. 9(B). Interestingly, all the GCD curves exhibit a symmetrical triangle shape, indicating a high degree of reversibility during charge and discharge process. Meanwhile, the rate performance of CQDs/PPy-NW against the current density is depicted in Fig. 9(C), the supercapacitor has a large areal capacitance of 248.5 mF cm−2 at 0.2 mA cm−2, with good rate capability when the current density is increased by 50 times (65.4%). Besides, the CQDs/PPy-NW exhibited a high areal capacitance values: 240 mF cm−2 at a low scan rate of 10 mV s−1. These obtained areal capacitance values are superior to those previously reported PPy-based nanocomposites, such as PPy/GO composite (152 mF cm−2, Ref. [16]), RGO-PPy membrane (175 mF cm−2, Ref. [27]), a PPy coated graphene electrode (151 mF cm−2, Ref. [40]) and PPy-GO/CNT (142.2 mF cm−2, Ref. [41]). It also outperforms other previously reported supercapacitor based on CQDs composites, such as active GQDs film (236 F g−1 at current density 0.5 A g−1, in three-electrode system, Ref. [20]), GQDs/CNT composite (55 mF cm−2 at current density 0.7 mA cm−2, in two-electrode system Ref. [28]) and C-dot decorated Fe3O4 electrode (208 F g−1 at current density 1 A g−1, in three-electrode system, Ref. [42]).

The Nyquist plots of impedance were collected in the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 000 Hz for CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor was shown Fig. 9(D). A high knee frequency was exhibited in the inset, showing that the low diffusion impendence and fast charge-transfer rate. Moreover, the Cre and Cim of specific capacitance as function of frequency of CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor was shown in Fig. 9(E). The Cim curve presented a sharp peak at a particular frequency (f 0 = 0.63 Hz), defining the shorter relaxation time of τ 0 = 1/f 0 = 1.6 s. This time constant indicated that the minimum time of CQDs/PPy-NW needed to discharge all the energy from the device with an efficiency of greater than 50% [43–45].

In addition, the cycle stability of the electrode materials is an imperative parameter to evaluate their potential for practical application. Fig. 9(F) demonstrated long-term cyclic life stability, as tested by GCD charge/discharge for CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite as electrode material. After 5000 continuous cycles, the as-prepared electrode material displays approximately 85.2% retention of its initial specific capacitance at a high current density of 5 mA cm−2, this value was high compared with other carbon-based nanomaterials involving 1D CNT (72% retention after 3000 continuous, Ref. [18]) and 2D graphene (84.8% retention after 5000 continuous, Ref. [36]). These results indicate that the CQDs-modified PPy-NW possesses good cycle-life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ragone plot of CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor device, ragone plots are demonstrated in Fig. 10 . Based on the total mass of electrode active material and as observed in Fig. 10(A), the energy density (ED) and power density (PD) of CQDs/PPy-NW reached 30.92 Wh kg−1 and 224 W kg−1, respectively. It preserves 62.4% of its ED as the PD increases to 11.2 kW kg−1. These results are better than those obtained in previous literature reports, such as CNT/PPy-NW nanocomposite (15.1 Wh kg−1 at 3000 W kg−1, Ref. [18]), activated carbon (28.4 Wh kg−1 at 400 W kg−1, Ref. [46]), PPy fibre/RGO films (20.6 Wh kg−1 at 1280 W kg−1, Ref. [15]), PANI fibre/RGO (16.7 Wh kg−1 at 986 W kg−1, Ref. [47]), 3D graphene/polymer (16.7 Wh kg−1 at 401 W kg−1, Ref. [48]) and 3D graphene/MnO2 composite networks (6.8 Wh kg−1 at 62 W kg−1, Ref. [49]). Furthermore, we also investigate the ED and PD on the geometric area of the CQDs/PPy-NW supercapacitor device. The results are shown in Fig. 10(B). As observed in the picture, when the current density increases from 0.2 to 10 mA cm−2, the ED decreases from 26.5 to 16.3 μW h cm−2 and the PD increases from 0.2 to 8.4 mW cm−2 for CQDs/PPy-NW, which is better than previous literature reports, such as PPy/graphene (3.15 μW h cm−2 at 5763 μW cm−2, Ref. [16]), PANI/graphite oxide (1.3 μWh cm−2 at 300 μW cm−2, Ref. [50]), MWCNTs@PEDOT/PSS (14.2 μWh cm−2 at 99.7 μW cm−2, Ref. [51]) and PEDOT/SDS-GO (4.88 μWh cm−2 at 7027 μW cm−2, Ref. [52]).

In summary, a simple electrostatic self-assembly strategy method has been proposed to fabricate CQDs/PPy-NW. Their electrochemical properties are studied. The electrochemical tests demonstrate that the as-made CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrode has a high specific capacitance of 306 F g−1 at current density of 0.5 A g−1. The as-fabricated symmetric supercapacitor with two CQDs/PPy-NW composite electrodes presents a large areal capacitance of 248.5 mF cm−2 at current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 and good cycling capability with ∼85.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles. These results are attributed to the CQDs/PPy-NW which provides larger surface area, more active edges and more conducive paths. Predictably, the CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite is a very promising electrode material for high-performance electrochemical energy storage devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Shenhua Group Corp. (Grant nos. U1261103), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China. (Grant No. 201601D011023), Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Fund Project of Shanxi. (No. 2016BY050) and the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (No. J12-13-913). The authors thank for anonymous reviewers for their helpful suggestions on the quality improvement of our present paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.12.033.
